spec_match.rationale: Page exercises the concept interactively but stops short of touching the real API surface — the underlying spec behaviour is simulated rather than invoked.

interactive.rationale: Real interactive controls drive the page (5 buttons, 0 inputs, 0 selects, 0 ranges, 6 listeners) — well past the static-card bar.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Concept page lede restates and delivers on the framing used in the feature index card.

edge_cases.rationale: Page surfaces multiple failure / unsupported / edge-case branches in the UI, not just the happy path.

references.rationale: Page cites the chromestatus.com/feature/5144837209194496 link and the spec, as required by CLAUDE.md rule 3.

design_system.rationale: Uses CSS variables throughout, no raw hex of note, and button overrides keep background + color paired (CLAUDE.md rules 5 and 6).

openQuestions.title: Exercise more of the SoftNavigation performance entry spec surface
openQuestions.detail: The spec (https://wicg.github.io/soft-navigations) covers more than the page demonstrates. Walk back through the spec's exposed methods, properties, or modes and add controls for the ones that materially change behaviour. Today the page only hints at a subset.

openQuestions.title: Mirror the spec's stated motivation in the demo
openQuestions.detail: ChromeStatus motivation reads: 'Web developers have been asking for a way to take "soft navigations" - JS-driven navigations in Single Page Apps (SPA) - into account when exposing performance metrics. This exposes a browser heuristic to that effect. This would enable dev...'. The demo should make that motivation visible — show the problem the spec is solving (before/after, with-without, attacker view) so a reader leaves understanding why the feature ships, not just what it does.

summary: 'Observer Playground' is a solid concept page: interactive surface lands, references are in place, and the design system rules hold. The follow-ups in openQuestions are polish, not blockers.
